texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: ABSTRACT
texts.text: ABSTRACT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymeric gas separation membrane has been widely used for a variety of industrial gas separation applications. This review presents the structure and property relation and describes the membrane fabrication technology. Polymers are believed to be used commercially for gas separation and hence the challenges associated with the development of membrane materials are also explained. Comparison of competitive technologies and membrane technology and some classes of polymers are also discussed in this review. The focus of the work also include Thermally Rearranged polymer (TR), Polymer of Intrinsic Micro porosity (PIMs), perfluro polymer and high performance polyimide.
texts.text: Polymeric gas separation membrane has been widely used for a variety of industrial gas separation applications. This review presents the structure and property relation and describes the membrane fabrication technology. Polymers are believed to be used commercially for gas separation and hence the challenges associated with the development of membrane materials are also explained. Comparison of competitive technologies and membrane technology and some classes of polymers are also discussed in this review. The focus of the work also include Thermally Rearranged polymer (TR), Polymer of Intrinsic Micro porosity (PIMs), perfluro polymer and high performance polyimide.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: Gas separation membrane, Polymer membrane, Thermally arranged polymer, High performance polyimide, Membrane technology.
texts.text: Keywords: Gas separation membrane, Polymer membrane, Thermally arranged polymer, High performance polyimide, Membrane technology.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: 1. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gas separation membrane market has grown significantly since its beginning in the 1970s, and continued growth is expected in the coming years as technology improves and applications expand. Previously, several review articles and monographs have chronicled overall progress in the eld as well as specific progress in applications and materials science. This review focuses on the role of polymer science in current and future materials for gas separation applications. To provide context for the materials science discussion, current applications of polymeric gas separation membranes are discussed alongwith potential future applications that may become feasible with improved membrane materials. Membrane performance is often characterized by gas throughput and separation efficiency and these properties are most commonly expressed by permeability and selectivity coefficients.
texts.text: The gas separation membrane market has grown significantly since its beginning in the 1970s, and continued growth is expected in the coming years as technology improves and applications expand. Previously, several review articles and monographs have chronicled overall progress in the eld as well as specific progress in applications and materials science. This review focuses on the role of polymer science in current and future materials for gas separation applications. To provide context for the materials science discussion, current applications of polymeric gas separation membranes are discussed alongwith potential future applications that may become feasible with improved membrane materials. Membrane performance is often characterized by gas throughput and separation efficiency and these properties are most commonly expressed by permeability and selectivity coefficients.

texts.text: For a pure gas permeating through a polymer film or membrane gas, permeability, PA , is dened by means of the trans-membrane pressure difference, p 2 to p 1, and thickness normalized steady-state gas flux, Na [1]

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: PA = Na l / ( p 2 -p 1 )

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.3. Fractional free volume
texts.text: 1.3. Fractional free volume

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Free volume is among the most important structural variables inuencing gas transport properties in polymers. The Cohen Turnbull model predicts that diffusion coefcients increase strongly as free volume increases. This relationship is shown below, where A is a constant related to the geometric factor, molecular diameter and gas kinetic velocity, v* is a parameter related to the size of the gas molecule, g is an overlap factor introduced to prevent double counting of free volume elements, and FFV is the fractional free volume of the polymer.
texts.text: Free volume is among the most important structural variables inuencing gas transport properties in polymers. The Cohen Turnbull model predicts that diffusion coefcients increase strongly as free volume increases. This relationship is shown below, where A is a constant related to the geometric factor, molecular diameter and gas kinetic velocity, v* is a parameter related to the size of the gas molecule, g is an overlap factor introduced to prevent double counting of free volume elements, and FFV is the fractional free volume of the polymer.

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: D = Aexp ( λ V ) FFV

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In gas separation polymers, free volume is the void space between polymer chain segments that is available to assist in molecular transport. Free volume can be generated by inefficiencies in polymer chain packing in the solid state and by molecular motion of polymer chain segments, which effectively open gaps in the polymer matrix on a transient basis that allow penetrant molecules to diffuse through the polymer. Fractional free volume is calculated as the difference between the experimental specic volume (i.e., reciprocal of polymer density) and the theoretical volume occupied by polymer chain [7].
texts.text: In gas separation polymers, free volume is the void space between polymer chain segments that is available to assist in molecular transport. Free volume can be generated by inefficiencies in polymer chain packing in the solid state and by molecular motion of polymer chain segments, which effectively open gaps in the polymer matrix on a transient basis that allow penetrant molecules to diffuse through the polymer. Fractional free volume is calculated as the difference between the experimental specic volume (i.e., reciprocal of polymer density) and the theoretical volume occupied by polymer chain [7].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.4. Plasticization
texts.text: 1.4. Plasticization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As the concentration of gas inside a polymer increases, the polymer can swell, which increases free volume and chain motion that, in turn, increases gas diffusion coefcients and decreases diffusion selectivity. This phenomenon is known as plasticization. Plasticization often results in higher gas ux but lower mixed gas selectivity, particularly at high pressures. One common signature of plasticization is the increase in permeability of a gas as the upstream partial pressure of that gas increases. However, strictly speaking, increase in permeability can be due to either increase in solubility, increase in diffusivity, or both, and plasticization is typically associated with increase in permeability driven by increase in gas diffusion coefcients as upstream pressure and, therefore, the concentration of gas dissolved in the polymer, increases [8]. Another common symptom of plasticization is an increase in permeability of all components in a mixture and a loss in selectivity as upstream total pressure or partial pressure of one or more components increases[9].
texts.text: As the concentration of gas inside a polymer increases, the polymer can swell, which increases free volume and chain motion that, in turn, increases gas diffusion coefcients and decreases diffusion selectivity. This phenomenon is known as plasticization. Plasticization often results in higher gas ux but lower mixed gas selectivity, particularly at high pressures. One common signature of plasticization is the increase in permeability of a gas as the upstream partial pressure of that gas increases. However, strictly speaking, increase in permeability can be due to either increase in solubility, increase in diffusivity, or both, and plasticization is typically associated with increase in permeability driven by increase in gas diffusion coefcients as upstream pressure and, therefore, the concentration of gas dissolved in the polymer, increases [8]. Another common symptom of plasticization is an increase in permeability of all components in a mixture and a loss in selectivity as upstream total pressure or partial pressure of one or more components increases[9].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: CO2 is a common gas used in plasticization studies [11]. Among gases of importance in gas separation applications, CO2 is often among the more soluble gases, and plasticization by CO2 is widely known and studied in relation CO2 removal from natural gas [10]. Many polymers sorb enough CO2 at accessible pressures to strongly plasticize, so it is a convenient penetrant for such studies.
texts.text: CO2 is a common gas used in plasticization studies [11]. Among gases of importance in gas separation applications, CO2 is often among the more soluble gases, and plasticization by CO2 is widely known and studied in relation CO2 removal from natural gas [10]. Many polymers sorb enough CO2 at accessible pressures to strongly plasticize, so it is a convenient penetrant for such studies.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. MEMBRANE FABRICATION AND COMPETING TECHNOLOGIES FOR GAS SEPARATION PROCESSES
texts.text: 2. MEMBRANE FABRICATION AND COMPETING TECHNOLOGIES FOR GAS SEPARATION PROCESSES

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Membrane fabrication
texts.text: 2.1. Membrane fabrication

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Today, all gas separation membranes are made by processes based on the concept of phase inversion because it is the only commercially viable way known for making it thin of the order of 100nm or less, defect-free membrane. To prepare membranes via phase inversion, a water-insoluble polymer is dissolved in a water miscible,
texts.text: Today, all gas separation membranes are made by processes based on the concept of phase inversion because it is the only commercially viable way known for making it thin of the order of 100nm or less, defect-free membrane. To prepare membranes via phase inversion, a water-insoluble polymer is dissolved in a water miscible,

texts.label: page_header
texts.orig: S.Walke et al./ Journal of Advances in Civil Engineering, Vol. 4(1), 2018 pp. 24-31
texts.text: S.Walke et al./ Journal of Advances in Civil Engineering, Vol. 4(1), 2018 pp. 24-31

texts.label: text
texts.orig: high boiling solvent or mixture of solvents; the resulting solution is often referred to as a polymer dope. This dope is then cast using a doctor blade onto a porous backing material, for at sheet, spiral wound membranes, or extruded through a hollow bre spinneret to prepare hollow bres [11]. After passing through a short air gap to allow solvent evaporation from the surface of the nascent membrane to begin formation of a thin, dense layer on the surface, the cast or extruded polymer dope is immersed in a non-solvent usually water bath. In this bath, solvent exchange and polymer coagulation produce a porous substrate with a thin, dense skin on top of it, in the case of spiral wound membranes, or on the outside of hollow bres. Often, the polymer dope concentration would be in the range of 30-35 wt%, and small amounts of a water- soluble polymer e.g., poly vinyl pyrrolidone or polyethylene oxide oligomers could be added to optimize the porous structure [6]. To reduce macro-void formation, water, alcohols or other additives are included in the original polymer dope, so that the dope is very close to the phase separation boundary before being cast or extruded [6].
texts.text: high boiling solvent or mixture of solvents; the resulting solution is often referred to as a polymer dope. This dope is then cast using a doctor blade onto a porous backing material, for at sheet, spiral wound membranes, or extruded through a hollow bre spinneret to prepare hollow bres [11]. After passing through a short air gap to allow solvent evaporation from the surface of the nascent membrane to begin formation of a thin, dense layer on the surface, the cast or extruded polymer dope is immersed in a non-solvent usually water bath. In this bath, solvent exchange and polymer coagulation produce a porous substrate with a thin, dense skin on top of it, in the case of spiral wound membranes, or on the outside of hollow bres. Often, the polymer dope concentration would be in the range of 30-35 wt%, and small amounts of a water- soluble polymer e.g., poly vinyl pyrrolidone or polyethylene oxide oligomers could be added to optimize the porous structure [6]. To reduce macro-void formation, water, alcohols or other additives are included in the original polymer dope, so that the dope is very close to the phase separation boundary before being cast or extruded [6].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Competing technologies/membrane competiveness
texts.text: 2.2. Competing technologies/membrane competiveness

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Often gas separation membranes are not the dominant technology for a given gas separation application. Cryogenic distillation, pressure (vacuum) swing adsorption, and chemical absorption processes dominate commercial gas separation processes.
texts.text: Often gas separation membranes are not the dominant technology for a given gas separation application. Cryogenic distillation, pressure (vacuum) swing adsorption, and chemical absorption processes dominate commercial gas separation processes.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Cryogenic distillation
texts.text: 2.3. Cryogenic distillation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cryogenic distillation is no different than conventional distillation except that the temperatures are considerably lower [12]. Plate and tray distillation columns are employed at cryogenic temperatures. As in conventional distillation, temperature differences from the bottom to the top of the column allow for separation. In the case of air separation, large quantities of air are compressed, puried, cooled and then liqueed before being separated in distillation columns [13].
texts.text: Cryogenic distillation is no different than conventional distillation except that the temperatures are considerably lower [12]. Plate and tray distillation columns are employed at cryogenic temperatures. As in conventional distillation, temperature differences from the bottom to the top of the column allow for separation. In the case of air separation, large quantities of air are compressed, puried, cooled and then liqueed before being separated in distillation columns [13].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition, cryogenic separation can be used to separate hydrogen from much more readily condensable hydrocarbons. For obvious reasons, cryogenic distillation is the method of choice for the large liquid oxygen and liquid nitrogen markets as well as applications requiring high purity.
texts.text: In addition, cryogenic separation can be used to separate hydrogen from much more readily condensable hydrocarbons. For obvious reasons, cryogenic distillation is the method of choice for the large liquid oxygen and liquid nitrogen markets as well as applications requiring high purity.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4. Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)
texts.text: 2.4. Pressure Swing Adsorption (PSA)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It involves pressurizing a gas mixture in the presence of specic adsorbents such as zeolites, silica, alumina and activated carbon [14]. PSA can operate at ambient conditions and thus the energy costs associated with heating or cooling is minimized. These adsorbents are porous structures with extremely high internal surface areas and can adsorb one gas preferentially to the other [14]. Molecular sieves can prevent a larger diameter gas from entering the pores and can thus provide separation based on gas molecule size [15]. Once pressurization is conducted, the system is depressurized, allowing the lower absorbing gas to leave the adsorption bed rst. This depressurization step leaves the chamber enriched in the gas with the higher sorption value on the adsorbent [16]. Usually, this process involves at least two beds because one is being pressurized i.e., is being used to adsorb gases and the other is being depressurized i.e., regenerated to allow continuous operation. If operated near ambient pressure for one cycle of the process and vacuum for the other cycle, the process is referred to as Vacuum Swing Adsorption (VSA). PSA is the primary process used for hydrogen purication [16, 17]. Reneries use PSA for hydrotreating and hydrocracking processes as well as the removal of H2S from hydrogen. PSA is also used to separate carbon dioxide from methane for natural gas purication.
texts.text: It involves pressurizing a gas mixture in the presence of specic adsorbents such as zeolites, silica, alumina and activated carbon [14]. PSA can operate at ambient conditions and thus the energy costs associated with heating or cooling is minimized. These adsorbents are porous structures with extremely high internal surface areas and can adsorb one gas preferentially to the other [14]. Molecular sieves can prevent a larger diameter gas from entering the pores and can thus provide separation based on gas molecule size [15]. Once pressurization is conducted, the system is depressurized, allowing the lower absorbing gas to leave the adsorption bed rst. This depressurization step leaves the chamber enriched in the gas with the higher sorption value on the adsorbent [16]. Usually, this process involves at least two beds because one is being pressurized i.e., is being used to adsorb gases and the other is being depressurized i.e., regenerated to allow continuous operation. If operated near ambient pressure for one cycle of the process and vacuum for the other cycle, the process is referred to as Vacuum Swing Adsorption (VSA). PSA is the primary process used for hydrogen purication [16, 17]. Reneries use PSA for hydrotreating and hydrocracking processes as well as the removal of H2S from hydrogen. PSA is also used to separate carbon dioxide from methane for natural gas purication.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.5. Comparison of PSA and cryogenic distillation
texts.text: 2.5. Comparison of PSA and cryogenic distillation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PSA are well suited for small scale oxygen production while cryogenic distillation is used for large scale oxygen production. PSA is the process of pressurizing and depressurizing gas to optimize gas separation. Cryogenic separation requires a combination of low temperature and high pressure to liquefy air to be separated by distillation. PSA are normally associated with capital when compared to other technology while cryogenic process require the use of numerous equipment like compressor turbine, heat exchanger, insulator and distillation column and maintenance of these equipment makes it capital intensive. A lower pressure or vacuum is used to desorb the adsorbent bed and PSA cycle can be operating close to isothermal condition without heating or cooling steps. For this reason PSA cycle is attractive to bulk separation process whereas cryogenic plant utilize cryogenic separation which requires a combination of low temperature and high pressure to liquify air to be separated by distillation. PSA is associated to low energy and cryogenic distillation is associated with high energy. PSA require low power

While on the other side, the view membrane are expensive, certain solvent can quickly and permanently destroy the membrane. Life cycle of adsorbent is more than 10 yrs. which is comparable to that of the membrane.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.6. Chemical absorption processes
texts.text: 2.6. Chemical absorption processes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Presently, the primary process to remove CO2 from natural gas and power plant flue gas involves a monoethanolamine chemical absorbent-based process. When 2an-amine is reacted with carbon dioxide, a nitrogen substituted carbamic acid is formed. This is a rather unstable product which will decompose to the original amine and carbon dioxide at modest temperatures (>100 o C). Thus amines can be used to capture CO2 at ambient temperatures and the amines can be regenerated and CO2 can be collected as a gas at higher temperatures [16]. This approach provides a facile method to separate CO2 from gas mixtures and collect CO2 at high purity. This process is widely used and has been proposed for the collection of CO2 for sequestration from power plant flue gas [20]. While monoethanolamine is one of the preferred amines used in this process, other similar amines such as diethanolamine, methyl diethanolamine and triethanolamine, which can also remove H2S from natural gas streams, have been employed [16].
texts.text: Presently, the primary process to remove CO2 from natural gas and power plant flue gas involves a monoethanolamine chemical absorbent-based process. When 2an-amine is reacted with carbon dioxide, a nitrogen substituted carbamic acid is formed. This is a rather unstable product which will decompose to the original amine and carbon dioxide at modest temperatures (>100 o C). Thus amines can be used to capture CO2 at ambient temperatures and the amines can be regenerated and CO2 can be collected as a gas at higher temperatures [16]. This approach provides a facile method to separate CO2 from gas mixtures and collect CO2 at high purity. This process is widely used and has been proposed for the collection of CO2 for sequestration from power plant flue gas [20]. While monoethanolamine is one of the preferred amines used in this process, other similar amines such as diethanolamine, methyl diethanolamine and triethanolamine, which can also remove H2S from natural gas streams, have been employed [16].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Drawbacks of membrane gas separation include limited ability to achieve high purity separation, lack of feasibility in large-scale operation and sometimes higher capital cost.
texts.text: Drawbacks of membrane gas separation include limited ability to achieve high purity separation, lack of feasibility in large-scale operation and sometimes higher capital cost.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. MEMBRANE PROCESS VERSES ALL OTHER TECHNOLOGIES
texts.text: 3. MEMBRANE PROCESS VERSES ALL OTHER TECHNOLOGIES

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As noted later, membrane separation processes have favourably competed with conventional processes for a number of reasons. Membrane processes are generally more energy efcient. It offer simplicity of operation and are favourable for smaller scale operations where gas purity is not critical. It considered a 'fragile' system based on 100nm dense lm thicknesses. Membranes have shown durability and reliability during long-term use even under continuous use with high pressure drop approaching 1000 psi (69 bar) in specic cases across the hollow bre or at membrane [21].

It offer simplicity of operation and are favourable for smaller scale operations where gas purity is not critical. It considered a 'fragile' system based on 100nm dense lm thicknesses. Membranes have shown durability and reliability during long-term use even under continuous use with high pressure drop approaching 1000 psi (69 bar) in specic cases across the hollow bre or at membrane [21].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. POLYMER MEMBRANES
texts.text: 4. POLYMER MEMBRANES

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Polysulphone Membrane (PS)
texts.text: 4.1. Polysulphone Membrane (PS)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Synthesis of Polysulfones can be prepared via nucleophilic aromatic polycondensation of an aromatic dihydroxy compound with a bis-(halophenyl) sulfone. It is the most chemically and thermally durable thermoplastic polymer with high rigidity (high Tg), high strength, good creep resistance, dimensional stability, and high heat deection temperature. Polysulfone remains widely used for hydrogen and air separations [22].
texts.text: The Synthesis of Polysulfones can be prepared via nucleophilic aromatic polycondensation of an aromatic dihydroxy compound with a bis-(halophenyl) sulfone. It is the most chemically and thermally durable thermoplastic polymer with high rigidity (high Tg), high strength, good creep resistance, dimensional stability, and high heat deection temperature. Polysulfone remains widely used for hydrogen and air separations [22].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Cellulose Acetate (CA)
texts.text: 4.2. Cellulose Acetate (CA)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: They are produced by acetylation of cellulose with a source of acetate esters, typically acetic anhydride or acetic acid, and a catalyst such as sulphuric acid.CA membranes are relatively inexpensive in part because cellulose, the raw material is an abundant and renewable resource. Applications-for the separation of CO2 from mixtures with hydrocarbons enhanced oil recovery operations for natural gas separation [23].
texts.text: They are produced by acetylation of cellulose with a source of acetate esters, typically acetic anhydride or acetic acid, and a catalyst such as sulphuric acid.CA membranes are relatively inexpensive in part because cellulose, the raw material is an abundant and renewable resource. Applications-for the separation of CO2 from mixtures with hydrocarbons enhanced oil recovery operations for natural gas separation [23].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. PPO
texts.text: 4.3. PPO

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is synthesized from substituted phenols via oxidative coupling polymerization which involves the roomtemperature oxidative CeO coupling of 2,6-dimethylphenol in the presence of stoichiometric amounts of oxygen.This process is catalyzed by CuCl and an amine ligand, such as pyridine. It was the rst commercialized aromatic polyether and is still being used in many industrial applications like gas separations [24]. The kinked ether linkage and the absence of polar groups suppress efcient chain packing and densication, resulting in a relatively large fractional free volume in PPO, as seen in table 1. The high free volume and the ease of rotation of the phenyl rings about the ether linkages contribute to high diffusivity and permeability [25].
texts.text: It is synthesized from substituted phenols via oxidative coupling polymerization which involves the roomtemperature oxidative CeO coupling of 2,6-dimethylphenol in the presence of stoichiometric amounts of oxygen.This process is catalyzed by CuCl and an amine ligand, such as pyridine. It was the rst commercialized aromatic polyether and is still being used in many industrial applications like gas separations [24]. The kinked ether linkage and the absence of polar groups suppress efcient chain packing and densication, resulting in a relatively large fractional free volume in PPO, as seen in table 1. The high free volume and the ease of rotation of the phenyl rings about the ether linkages contribute to high diffusivity and permeability [25].

texts.text: Table A1.Permeability of common gases and fractional free volume

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.4. Aramids
texts.text: 4.4. Aramids

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Syntheses by Aromatic polyamides, or aramids, are generally produced from step polymerization or the polycondensation reaction of aromatic diamines with aromatic di-acid chlorides. The advantage is that the wholly aromatic structures of the aramid polymers give them excellent thermal and mechanical properties, which make them useful in technologies such as gas separations [26].
texts.text: Syntheses by Aromatic polyamides, or aramids, are generally produced from step polymerization or the polycondensation reaction of aromatic diamines with aromatic di-acid chlorides. The advantage is that the wholly aromatic structures of the aramid polymers give them excellent thermal and mechanical properties, which make them useful in technologies such as gas separations [26].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.5. Polycarbonate (PC)
texts.text: 4.5. Polycarbonate (PC)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Synthesis-Polycarbonates are polyesters of carbonic acid (especially derived from phosgene or diphenyl carbonate) which enhances the chain packing contributes to increase O2/N2 selectivity in the polymer, making it more attractive for use as an air separation membrane [27].
texts.text: Synthesis-Polycarbonates are polyesters of carbonic acid (especially derived from phosgene or diphenyl carbonate) which enhances the chain packing contributes to increase O2/N2 selectivity in the polymer, making it more attractive for use as an air separation membrane [27].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.6. Polyimides Matrimid (PIM)
texts.text: 4.6. Polyimides Matrimid (PIM)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Its synthesis by Aromatic polyimides is typically by means of the polycondensation products of aromatic dianhydride and aromatic diamine monomers. In a classic method of polyimide synthesis, a tetracarboxylic acid di-anhydride is added to a solution of diamine in a polar aprotic solvent at relatively low temperatures (15-75 o C). The advantage is that, it has high gas permeability and high intrinsic selectivity combined with desirable physical properties. It's used for gas separation. It has the best CO2 permeability, CO2/CH4 selectivity and high pure gas permeability than polysulphone and polycarbonate. The main disadvantage is plasticization [28].
texts.text: Its synthesis by Aromatic polyimides is typically by means of the polycondensation products of aromatic dianhydride and aromatic diamine monomers. In a classic method of polyimide synthesis, a tetracarboxylic acid di-anhydride is added to a solution of diamine in a polar aprotic solvent at relatively low temperatures (15-75 o C). The advantage is that, it has high gas permeability and high intrinsic selectivity combined with desirable physical properties. It's used for gas separation. It has the best CO2 permeability, CO2/CH4 selectivity and high pure gas permeability than polysulphone and polycarbonate. The main disadvantage is plasticization [28].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. EXPECTED OUTCOME 5.1. Matrimid
texts.text: 5. EXPECTED OUTCOME 5.1. Matrimid

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Matrimid is more expensive than many other commercial polymers. It may plasticide when exposed to gas stream with condensable components like CO2. Plasticization results due to CO2 and CH4 permeability increases and significant loss in CO2/CH4 selectivity decreases. So it needs high performance polymer that maintains separation characteristics in the presence of plasticization. So the innovative idea is the need to modify matrimid membrane via thermal treatment annealing or chemical cross linking to suppress plasticization [29].
texts.text: Matrimid is more expensive than many other commercial polymers. It may plasticide when exposed to gas stream with condensable components like CO2. Plasticization results due to CO2 and CH4 permeability increases and significant loss in CO2/CH4 selectivity decreases. So it needs high performance polymer that maintains separation characteristics in the presence of plasticization. So the innovative idea is the need to modify matrimid membrane via thermal treatment annealing or chemical cross linking to suppress plasticization [29].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.2. PIM-(Intrinsic micro porosity in polymers)
texts.text: 5.2. PIM-(Intrinsic micro porosity in polymers)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is a continuous network of interconnected intermolecular voids which forms a direct consequence on the shape and rigidity of the component macromolecules. PIM which in the solid state behave like molecular sieves. As pervaporation membranes, they show selectivity for organics over water. As gas separation membranes, they exhibit a remarkable combination of high permeability and good selectivity for gas pairs such as O2/N2 [30, 31].
texts.text: It is a continuous network of interconnected intermolecular voids which forms a direct consequence on the shape and rigidity of the component macromolecules. PIM which in the solid state behave like molecular sieves. As pervaporation membranes, they show selectivity for organics over water. As gas separation membranes, they exhibit a remarkable combination of high permeability and good selectivity for gas pairs such as O2/N2 [30, 31].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.3. TR polymer
texts.text: 5.3. TR polymer

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It is the thermal rearrangement of ortho functional aromatic polyimide
texts.text: It is the thermal rearrangement of ortho functional aromatic polyimide

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It increases free volume element and make size distribution uniform.
texts.text: It increases free volume element and make size distribution uniform.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · It increases solubility and diffusivity and diffusivity provide increase in gas permeability.
texts.text: It increases solubility and diffusivity and diffusivity provide increase in gas permeability.

texts.text: S.Walke et al./ Journal of Advances in Civil Engineering, Vol. 4(1), 2018 pp. 24-31

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are 3 major benefits of TR membrane
texts.text: There are 3 major benefits of TR membrane

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. High combination of permeability and selectivity i.e. High CO2 permeability and good CO2/CH4 permselectivity .
texts.text: High combination of permeability and selectivity i.e. High CO2 permeability and good CO2/CH4 permselectivity .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Resistance to plasticization (i.e. excellent resistance to CO2 induced plasticization) .
texts.text: Resistance to plasticization (i.e. excellent resistance to CO2 induced plasticization) .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. High chemical resistance [32].
texts.text: High chemical resistance [32].

texts.text: When blending PF with TR it is observed that

texts.label: list_item

texts.text: Increase N2/CH4 gas separation performance.

texts.orig: 6. CONCLUSION
texts.text: 6. CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are expanded viability of current commercial membrane material such as PPO, Aramid, Polyamide, PC, CA which have gas transport properties below new developing membrane with some plasticization.
texts.text: There are expanded viability of current commercial membrane material such as PPO, Aramid, Polyamide, PC, CA which have gas transport properties below new developing membrane with some plasticization.
